middleware.title: Middleware
middleware.description: Middleware runs before a request is completed, letting you modify the response by rewriting, redirecting, or adding headers. It executes on every matched route at the edge.
middleware.howItWorksDescription: Create a middleware.ts (or .js) file at the root of your project (or inside src/). It runs before every matched request.
middleware.matcherConfigDescription: The matcher determines which routes trigger your middleware. Without it, middleware runs on every request.
middleware.authChecksDesc: Verify tokens or session cookies before allowing access to protected routes. Redirect to login if unauthenticated.
middleware.geoRoutingDesc: Read request.geo to detect the user's country and rewrite to region-specific content or redirect to localized domains.
middleware.abTestingDesc: Assign users to experiment groups via cookies, then rewrite to different page variants without the user seeing a different URL.
middleware.rateLimitingDesc: Track request counts per IP using edge-compatible stores and return 429 responses when limits are exceeded.
middleware.proxyBoxDesc: Next.js 16 introduces proxy.ts as a complementary approach for certain use cases that were previously handled by middleware, such as proxying API requests or rewriting to external services. Middleware still works and is the right choice for auth checks, header modifications, and redirects. Check the Next.js docs for guidance on when to use each.
middleware.runsAtEdgeDesc: Middleware uses the Edge Runtime by default. You cannot use Node.js-only APIs like fs or native modules.
middleware.oneFileDesc: There is only one middleware.ts per project. Use the matcher config or conditional logic inside the function to handle different routes.

i18n.title: Internationalization (i18n)
i18n.description: This project uses next-intl for internationalization with a cookie-based locale strategy. Switch languages using the locale switcher in the sidebar footer.
i18n.routingStrategiesDescription: next-intl supports multiple strategies for determining the active locale.
i18n.localePrefixDesc: URLs include the locale: /en/about, /zh-TW/about. Requires a [locale] dynamic segment wrapping your routes. Best for SEO and shareable localized URLs.
i18n.cookieBasedDesc: The locale is stored in a cookie. URLs stay clean (/about) and the server reads the cookie to determine which translations to load. Simpler routing, no URL prefixes needed.
i18n.serverComponentsDesc: In Server Components, use the async getTranslations function to access translations without client-side JavaScript.
i18n.clientComponentsDesc: In Client Components, use the useTranslations hook. The component must be wrapped by NextIntlClientProvider (set up in providers).
i18n.providerSetupDesc: The NextIntlClientProvider in the root layout passes messages and locale to all client components.
i18n.tryItNowDesc: Use the locale switcher in the sidebar footer to toggle between English and Traditional Chinese. Watch the UI update instantly.
i18n.typeSafetyDesc: next-intl supports TypeScript autocompletion for translation keys. Define your message types to catch missing translations at build time.

auth.title: Authentication
auth.description: Authentication patterns in Next.js cover session management, route protection, and integration with popular auth libraries.
auth.jwtSessionsDesc: Stateless tokens stored in cookies. No database lookup needed on each request. Fast but harder to revoke. Good for simple apps and edge deployments.
auth.dbSessionsDesc: Session ID stored in a cookie, session data in a database. Easy to revoke and manage. Requires a database query per request. Better for apps needing strict session control.
auth.protectingRoutesDescription: There are two main approaches to protect routes from unauthenticated users.
auth.authInterruptsDesc: Next.js 16 introduces authInterrupts, a built-in way to handle authorization errors with dedicated error boundaries. Enable it in your config, then create forbidden.tsx and unauthorized.tsx files.
auth.authJsDesc: Full-featured auth with OAuth providers, credentials, JWT and database sessions. Deep Next.js integration.
auth.clerkDesc: Hosted auth service with drop-in UI components, user management dashboard, and webhook support.
auth.luciaDesc: Minimal, session-based auth library. You own the database schema. Good for learning and custom setups.
auth.middlewareVsServerDesc: Middleware is fast (edge) but limited — good for redirects. Server Component checks are more flexible — you can query databases and render different UI.
auth.neverTrustClientDesc: Always verify auth on the server. Client-side checks (hiding UI) are for UX only. Server Components and Server Actions must independently verify permissions.

draftMode.title: Draft Mode
draftMode.description: Draft Mode lets you bypass static rendering to preview unpublished content from your CMS. When enabled, pages are rendered dynamically on each request.
draftMode.howItWorksDescription: Draft Mode sets a special cookie that tells Next.js to render pages dynamically instead of serving the static version. This is perfect for CMS preview workflows.
draftMode.readingDesc: Check whether draft mode is active in any Server Component to conditionally fetch published or draft content.
draftMode.step1: Configure your CMS to generate a preview URL: /api/draft?secret=MY_SECRET&slug=/blog/my-post
draftMode.step2: The API route validates the secret, calls draftMode().enable(), and redirects to the target page.
draftMode.step3: The page detects draft mode and fetches unpublished content from the CMS.
draftMode.step4: The editor reviews the preview and can exit by hitting the disable endpoint.
draftMode.cookieBasedDesc: Draft mode uses a special __prerender_bypass cookie. It persists across page navigations until explicitly disabled or the cookie expires.
draftMode.securityDesc: Always validate a secret token before enabling draft mode. Without it, anyone could access unpublished content by hitting your API route directly.
draftMode.dynamicRenderingDesc: When draft mode is enabled, the page is rendered dynamically on every request. Static caching is bypassed completely for that user.
draftMode.worksWithAnyCmsDesc: Contentful, Sanity, Strapi, WordPress, or any headless CMS that supports preview URLs can integrate with Next.js draft mode.

edgeRuntime.title: Edge Runtime
edgeRuntime.description: The Edge Runtime is a lightweight subset of Node.js APIs designed for low-latency responses. Routes and middleware can opt into it for faster cold starts and global distribution.
edgeRuntime.optingInDesc: Export a runtime config from any route segment to switch from the default Node.js runtime to Edge.
edgeRuntime.edgeItem3: Web standard APIs (fetch, Response, URL, crypto)
edgeRuntime.nodeItem1: Full Node.js API access (fs, path, etc.)
edgeRuntime.nodeItem4: Runs in a single region by default
edgeRuntime.whatYouCanUseTitle: What You Can Use at the Edge
edgeRuntime.limitItem3: Native Node.js modules (e.g., bcrypt) — use Web Crypto or edge-compatible alternatives
edgeRuntime.limitItem4: Some npm packages that depend on Node.js internals
edgeRuntime.limitItem5: Long-running computations — edge functions have execution time limits
edgeRuntime.lowLatencyDesc: API routes that need fast global responses — feature flags, A/B test assignments, simple data lookups from edge-compatible stores like KV or D1.
edgeRuntime.geoRoutingDesc: Read the user's location from request headers and serve region-specific content or redirect to the nearest data center.
edgeRuntime.authTokenDesc: Validate JWTs at the edge using Web Crypto before the request reaches your origin server. Reject invalid tokens early.
edgeRuntime.personalizationDesc: Read cookies or headers to personalize responses without a round-trip to the origin. Combine with edge-compatible databases for dynamic content.
edgeRuntime.middlewareAlwaysEdgeDesc: middleware.ts always runs on the Edge Runtime. You do not need to export a runtime config — it is edge by default.
edgeRuntime.chooseWiselyDesc: Only use Edge Runtime when latency matters and your code stays within the API constraints. For most pages and APIs, the Node.js runtime is simpler and more capable.

errorHandling.title: Error Handling
errorHandling.description: Gracefully handle runtime errors with error.tsx boundaries — catch, display, and recover from errors without crashing your entire app.
errorHandling.errorTsxConventionDescription: Next.js uses React Error Boundaries under the hood. An error.tsx file automatically wraps a route segment and its children.
errorHandling.importantNote: error.tsx must be a Client Component ("use client"). Error boundaries use React's class component lifecycle which only works on the client.
errorHandling.globalErrorDescription: Catches errors in the root layout itself. It must render its own {'<'}html{'>'} and {'<'}body{'>'} tags since it replaces the root layout when active.
errorHandling.howResetWorksDesc1: The reset() function attempts to re-render the error boundary's children. If the error was transient (e.g., a network hiccup), the re-render may succeed without the error recurring.
errorHandling.howResetWorksDesc2: In production, error.digest provides a hash of the error for server-side log correlation without exposing sensitive details to the client.
errorHandling.triggerDesc: Click the button to throw an error. The error.tsx boundary will catch it and show recovery UI.
errorHandling.clientComponentRequiredDesc: error.tsx must start with "use client" — React error boundaries only work as client components.
errorHandling.nestedBoundariesDesc: Errors bubble up to the nearest error boundary. More specific boundaries prevent the entire page from being replaced.
errorHandling.layoutErrorsDesc: error.tsx does NOT catch errors in the same segment's layout.tsx. Use the parent segment's error.tsx or global-error.tsx instead.
errorHandling.productionDigestDesc: In production, error.digest gives a hashed identifier for server-side log matching without leaking error details.

mdx.title: MDX
mdx.description: Write JSX in your Markdown — combine the simplicity of content authoring with the power of React components for interactive documentation and blog posts.
mdx.whatIsMdxDescription: MDX lets you use JSX directly in Markdown files. You can import and use React components alongside standard Markdown content.
mdx.whatIsMdxNote: MDX is a superset of Markdown. Any valid Markdown is valid MDX, but you can also embed JSX expressions, import statements, and export metadata.
mdx.setupDesc: Install @next/mdx and configure your next.config.ts to handle .mdx files as pages or imports.
mdx.fileConventionDesc: MDX files in the app directory work just like page.tsx files. You can also import .mdx files as components.
mdx.mdxComponentsDesc: Define a mdx-components.tsx file at the project root to customize how standard HTML elements are rendered in MDX content.
mdx.serverComponentsDesc: MDX pages in the App Router are Server Components. Add "use client" as an export if you need interactivity.
mdx.remarkRehypeDesc: Extend MDX with remark plugins (Markdown AST) and rehype plugins (HTML AST) for features like syntax highlighting or math rendering.
mdx.typeSafetyDesc: Props passed to components in MDX files are type-checked. Your IDE will show errors if you pass the wrong props.
mdx.remoteMdxDesc: For MDX from a CMS or database, use next-mdx-remote or mdx-bundler to compile MDX at runtime with full component support.

instrumentation.title: Instrumentation
instrumentation.description: Monitor and trace your Next.js application with the instrumentation.ts convention — integrate OpenTelemetry, custom logging, and performance monitoring.
instrumentation.conventionDescription: Place an instrumentation.ts file at the project root (or inside src/). Next.js calls its register() function once when the server starts.
instrumentation.registerDesc: Export a register function that runs once when a new Next.js server instance is initialized. Use it to set up monitoring SDKs, loggers, or tracing.
instrumentation.openTelemetryDesc: Next.js has built-in support for OpenTelemetry. Install @vercel/otel for the simplest setup, or configure the OpenTelemetry SDK directly.
instrumentation.onRequestErrorDesc: Export an onRequestError function to capture and report server-side errors to your observability platform.
instrumentation.loggingDesc: Initialize structured logging (e.g., pino, winston) once at startup instead of on every request.
instrumentation.apmDesc: Set up Datadog, New Relic, or Sentry SDKs that need to instrument the process before any requests are handled.
instrumentation.tracingDesc: Configure OpenTelemetry to trace requests across services with automatic span creation for fetch, database calls, and route handlers.
instrumentation.featureFlagsDesc: Initialize feature flag SDKs (LaunchDarkly, Statsig) at startup so flags are available in all server components and middleware.
instrumentation.runtimeCheckDesc: Use process.env.NEXT_RUNTIME to conditionally load Node.js-only code. Edge runtime has limited API support.
instrumentation.runsOnceDesc: register() is called once per server instance, not per request. Use it for initialization, not per-request logic.
instrumentation.dynamicImportDesc: Use dynamic import() inside register() to avoid loading heavy dependencies in environments that don't need them.

pwa.title: Progressive Web App (PWA)
pwa.description: Make your Next.js app installable and offline-capable with web manifests, service workers, and PWA libraries like @serwist/next.
pwa.whatMakesPwaDescription: A Progressive Web App needs three things: a web manifest for installability, a service worker for offline support, and HTTPS.
pwa.webManifestBoxDesc: JSON file declaring app name, icons, theme color, and display mode so the browser can install it.
pwa.serviceWorkerBoxDesc: Background script that intercepts network requests, enabling offline access and caching strategies.
pwa.httpsBoxDesc: Required for service workers and secure context APIs. Vercel and most hosts provide this automatically.
pwa.webManifestSectionDesc: In the App Router, you can generate a manifest using a manifest.ts file or place a static manifest.json in the app directory.
pwa.serwistSetupDesc: @serwist/next (successor to next-pwa) provides automatic service worker generation with Workbox under the hood.
pwa.cacheFirstDesc: Serve from cache, fall back to network. Best for static assets like images, fonts, and CSS.
pwa.networkFirstDesc: Try network first, fall back to cache. Best for API responses and pages that need fresh data.
pwa.staleWhileRevalidateDesc: Serve from cache immediately while fetching an update in the background. Balances speed and freshness.
pwa.devVsProdDesc: Service workers are typically disabled in development. Test PWA features with a production build using next build && next start.
pwa.appShellDesc: Pre-cache the app shell (layout, navigation) so the app loads instantly even offline, then fetch dynamic content.
pwa.updateFlowDesc: Service workers update in the background. Use skipWaiting and clientsClaim for immediate activation, or prompt users to refresh.
pwa.lighthouseDesc: Use Chrome DevTools Lighthouse to audit your PWA score. It checks manifest, service worker, HTTPS, and offline capability.

dates.title: Date Handling with dayjs
dates.description: Lightweight date library (2KB) for formatting, parsing, relative time, and i18n — a modern alternative to Moment.js.
dates.whyDayjsDescription: dayjs provides a Moment.js-compatible API at a fraction of the size, with immutable operations and a plugin architecture.
dates.twoKbDesc: Moment.js is ~70KB. dayjs gives the same API at 2KB. Massive bundle size savings for client-side usage.
dates.immutableDesc: All operations return new dayjs objects. No accidental mutation of dates unlike the native Date or Moment.js.
dates.pluginSystemDesc: Only load what you need — relativeTime, timezone, weekday, and 20+ other plugins available on demand.
dates.serverDesc: Use dayjs in Server Components for static date formatting. The formatted string is sent as HTML — no JS shipped to the client.
dates.clientDesc: Use dayjs in Client Components for live timestamps, countdowns, or user-timezone-aware formatting. The 2KB library is included in the client bundle.
dates.tipDesc: For timezone handling, use the dayjs/plugin/utc and dayjs/plugin/timezone plugins. Detect user timezone with Intl.DateTimeFormat().resolvedOptions().timeZone.
dates.nativePitfallsDesc: Months are 0-indexed (January = 0), no built-in formatting, mutable by default, and timezone handling is painful.
dates.dayjsAdvantagesDesc: Consistent API, chainable operations, built-in formatting tokens, immutable, and locale support via plugins.
